task: After users post product reviews, customers in Asia-Pacific regions see 'No reviews yet' for 3-5 seconds while US users see them instantly. Manual refresh fixes it immediately.
rationale: Task uses 'stale data' language that triggers edge-cache or browser-cache routing, but the ROOT CAUSE is replicas in regional datacenters lagging behind the primary write. The 'eventually consistent' feel is *database replication lag*, not CDN/HTTP layer staleness.

task: A user updates their profile photo and immediately refreshes the page. The page still displays the old photo for 30 seconds. When testing the API directly, it returns fresh data immediately. Hard refresh (Ctrl+Shift+R) doesn't help.
rationale: The ruling-out clue 'API returns fresh data immediately' eliminates read-replica-lag (DB staleness). The clue 'hard refresh doesn't help' eliminates http-client-caching (browser cache). The staleness persists for 30 seconds at the layer between browser and API origin — the CDN edge cache. cdn-cache-invalidation is correct; the edge must be purged or TTL shortened.

task: A Stripe subscription_renewed webhook increments an internal 'active_subscriptions' counter on each delivery. Due to a 500 error, Stripe retries the webhook 3 times. The Stripe charge succeeds exactly once (correct), but the counter is now +3 instead of +1.
rationale: The ruling-out clue 'charge succeeds once but counter is +3' is critical: Stripe ensures charge idempotency (only one transaction), but the HTTP webhook payload is re-delivered 3 times. The problem is HTTP webhook replay, not the payment API integration or stream processing. webhook-idempotency solves HTTP event deduplication via idempotency keys. payment-gateway-integration addresses charge creation, not webhook re-delivery. streaming-exactly-once is for Kafka, not HTTP webhooks.

task: A GPU inference server batches 4 requests per forward pass, achieving 40 inferences/second throughput. GPU VRAM is 12% utilized. Batch latency is constant at 200ms. Customer needs 300 inferences/second.
rationale: The ruling-out clue 'GPU VRAM is 12% utilized' directly rules out gpu-oom-inference (which requires high memory pressure). Constant latency suggests the bottleneck is throughput, not latency variance. To increase throughput from 40 to 300 inferences/sec with low memory utilization, increase batch size and/or implement dynamic batching to saturate GPU. inference-batching-throughput is correct. model-quantization reduces model size, not throughput when VRAM is underutilized. distributed-tracing is observability, not a performance fix.

task: Rolling out a redesigned payment form to users. Plan: test with 12% of inbound traffic first, measure conversion rate, and toggle back to the old form instantly if conversions drop by >2%. The remaining 88% must see the old form during the test.
rationale: The ruling-out clues '12% of traffic' + 'instant kill-switch via flag' define a canary/percentage rollout pattern. blue-green-deploy is all-or-nothing traffic cutover (not gradual). hpa-autoscaling is about scaling pod replicas based on load, not feature rollout. pod-disruption-budget is about graceful pod termination during maintenance. feature-flag-rollout (canary / gradual rollout with instant rollback) is the only match.

task: Your search returns 'typo matches' ranked above 'exact keyword matches', even after tuning the embedding model recall. Keyword scores are systematically lower than vector scores in your ranked list.
rationale: The problem is not a single index (vector or keyword) but the MERGE. tuning HNSW won't fix keyword scores, tuning BM25 won't fix vector scores. Only reciprocal-rank-fusion (or custom weighting) re-ranks the combined results. A mid-level engineer might jump to 'tune the vector index' or 'tune BM25' because those are familiar levers, but the real issue is that two retrieval systems need to be married.

task: Your API endpoint returns fresh prices (verified in logs), but users via CloudFront see 10-minute-old prices. HTTP headers and database replication lag look fine.
rationale: The API returns fresh data; staleness is in the CDN/edge layer, not the browser or database. http-client-caching would manifest as the *browser* holding old data without refetch. read-replica-lag would show stale data in the logs themselves. cdn-cache-invalidation requires a purge or TTL update on the CDN layer.

task: Your ML inference model scores 100ms in a test script, but in production takes 8s with p99 at 45s. Profiling shows CUDA memory fragmentation, occasional GC pauses, GPU at 40% utilization.
rationale: The symptom is memory fragmentation + GC pauses (OOM pressure), not throughput. inference-batching would increase throughput but wouldn't fix the memory fragmentation and GC stops. model-quantization would shrink the model but you're already not OOMing (40% utilization). feature-train-serve-skew is model quality, not latency. Only gpu-oom-inference addresses the memory pressure → GC → latency spike.

task: Vector search recall dropped from 0.92 to 0.58 after switching embedding model from OpenAI (1536-dim) to a cheaper local model (384-dim). Tuning HNSW parameters (ef_search, ef_construction) does not recover recall.
rationale: embedding-model-selection is the only skill for choosing the right model (dimensions, domain fit, semantic space). vector-hnsw-tuning optimizes index parameters for a given model (can't fix wrong model). hybrid-search-fusion blends keyword+vector rankings (doesn't replace broken embeddings). metrics-cardinality handles time-series dimensionality (unrelated).

task: A GraphQL query `user(id: 1) { comments { id text } }` returns in 200ms solo but times out (>30s) when 100 concurrent users run it. Monitoring shows zero DB connection-pool exhaustion; query logs show 1000s of small queries.
rationale: graphql-n-plus-one diagnoses the query structure firing 1 + N queries (1 user + N comments). database-connection-pooling handles pool exhaustion (not applicable; monitoring shows no exhaustion). hpa-autoscaling scales pod replicas (doesn't reduce query cardinality). grpc-deadline-propagation manages gRPC timeouts (not GraphQL).

task: Your ML inference service crashes with CUDA out-of-memory at p95 load (batch=32), and predictions accuracy suddenly dropped 15% after retraining yesterday despite validation metrics looking good. Historical logs show training features joined at T, serving pipeline runs at T+5min. Diagnose both issues.
rationale: gpu-oom-inference diagnoses the CUDA OOM; feature-train-serve-skew explains the accuracy drop (temporal skew between training and serving features). Traps: inference-batching-throughput is about raising throughput, not OOM; model-quantization could reduce memory but misses the skew problem; data-backfill-strategy is about reprocessing historical data, not real-time feature pipeline desync.

task: Users sign up via OAuth and receive JWT tokens. You're rotating the JWT signing key tomorrow to comply with a policy, but early adopters report seeing their freshly-created profile data as missing momentarily (appears 3 seconds later). Both authentication and data visibility need fixing.
rationale: jwt-key-rotation handles rolling new signing keys without invalidating sessions; read-replica-lag addresses the read-after-write inconsistency on replicas. Traps: oauth-pkce is the OAuth protocol, not key rotation; cdn-cache-invalidation is about edge caches, not DB replication; http-client-caching is browser-side, not backend.

task: Your Kafka consumer fell 10M messages behind during a 2-hour outage. You need to catch up without double-counting events, and simultaneously backfill last week's data through a new dimension column that was just added. Which two skills recover both?
rationale: streaming-exactly-once ensures the 10M backlog replay doesn't duplicate events via checkpoints/transactional sinks; data-backfill-strategy safely reprocesses historical data in chunks. Traps: kafka-consumer-lag measures and alerts on lag but doesn't recover idempotently; webhook-idempotency is HTTP dedup; read-replica-lag is DB consistency, not stream processing.

task: Our distributed inventory sync cron runs every minute on Kubernetes; it often fires on two pods at once, causing lost updates and conflicting writes in the database—transactions abort with lock timeout errors. How do we ensure it only runs once?
rationale: The phrase 'lock timeout' and 'transaction abort' closely mirror deadlock error messages, making database-deadlocks tempting. But the root cause isn't a deadlock (two txns in a DB holding rows each ne

task: An attacker stole a user's authentication cookie and crafted a POST from their browser to transfer all funds without the user's action. The server accepted it. Our TLS and secret keys are fine. What security control fails here?
rationale: The terms 'authentication', 'token', and 'signature' pull toward webhook-signature-verification or jwt-key-rotation, but those are about verifying request origin/authenticity at API level. rate-limiti

task: Users report that the payment form is displaying stale card details from weeks ago. Refreshing (Ctrl+F5) fixes it. Every time we redeploy payment logic, some users see old cached content for hours. Where is the caching misconfiguration?
rationale: The mention of 'payment' and 'card' tempts you toward payment-gateway-integration (integrating Stripe). 'Caching' also suggests database-connection-pooling or cdn-cache-invalidation, but the symptom i

task: Search results miss synonyms even after tuning ANN recall to 95%. Users search 'car' but get exact 'automobile' matches only sporadically.
rationale: Lexical ranker sees 'recall' and 'ANN' and flags vector-hnsw-tuning, but the real issue is two independent retrieval paths (BM25 catching exact 'automobile', vector catching semantic 'car') that never

task: Full-text search for 'python' returns snakes and wildlife; vector search correctly ranks programming docs first. They never appear together in same result page.
rationale: Lexical ranker splits 'full-text search' to fulltext-bm25-tuning and 'vector search' to vector-hnsw-tuning. But both indices work correctly—the issue is they're run in separate queries and never fused

task: Event stream processes with 90-second latency; each event appears in analytics reports delayed by next batch window, causing SLA misses.
rationale: Ranker sees 'event stream' + 'delayed appear' and thinks read-replica-lag or train-serve-skew (offline/online gap). But Kafka consumer lag is specifically about partition assignment and throughput per

task: Stripe retries a webhook when our endpoint is slow, and we end up charging the customer twice. How do we make processing safe under those retries?
rationale: Double-charge under provider RETRIES = idempotency/dedup, not the payment API or signature auth.

task: Prometheus started OOMing right after we added a per-user_id label to one of our counters.
rationale: Unbounded label -> timeseries explosion = metrics cardinality, not logging/tracing.

task: Our Kafka consumers fall behind during peak and the lag keeps growing.
rationale: Kafka consumer lag, not DB replica lag (lexical 'lag' collision).

task: We're shipping a new ranking model: we need to shrink it to fit the GPU and serve it to 10% of traffic first.
rationale: Two intents: shrink model (quantization) + 10% rollout (feature-flag); GPU-OOM/batching are neighbors.

task: A search for 'python' on a full-text (inverted-index) search returns results ranked by term frequency: [python_internal.h, pythonic_utils, python (docs), Python Programming]. Relevance is poor — exact matches should rank higher. No semantic/embedding index exists.
rationale: The ruling-out clue 'full-text / inverted-index' + 'no semantic index' eliminates vector-hnsw-tuning and hybrid-search-fusion (which require embeddings). The problem is ranking relevance within lexical search. BM25 tuning (adjusting k1/b, analyzers, stemming) directly addresses term-frequency bias. fulltext-bm25-tuning is the solution. graphql-n-plus-one is about query multiplicity, not ranking.

task: Our ML model achieves 95% accuracy on the validation set but makes poor predictions in production on identical input distributions.
rationale: Train/serve skew happens when features are computed differently at training (batch aggregations) vs. serving (real-time), causing distribution mismatch. Quantization compresses the model size. GPU OOM is resource exhaustion. Batching improves throughput, not accuracy.

task: Two users simultaneously edit the same inventory item within 100ms. The first DB transaction aborts with 'deadlock detected', and the retry succeeds but silently overwrites the second user's quantity change (lost update). Both concurrency problems need fixing.
rationale: database-deadlocks addresses the deadlock via consistent lock ordering and retries; optimistic-concurrency-control prevents lost updates via version/timestamp checks. Traps: distributed-lock-redis is for cluster-wide mutual exclusion, not single-node transaction ordering; read-replica-lag is replication consistency; streaming-exactly-once is stream idempotency.

task: Vector ANN search has 65% recall on semantic benchmarks despite embedding model scoring 0.92 on eval suite. Latency is acceptable.
rationale: Lexical ranker flags 'embedding model' and assumes you picked the wrong one (embedding-model-selection). But the model is proven good offline. Low recall on index = underconfigured ef_search/ef_constr

task: A GraphQL resolver fetches 100 blog posts in 200ms, but the page takes 12 seconds to render. Profiling shows 101 SQL queries: 1 to fetch all posts + 100 queries executed inside the resolver loop (one per post to fetch the author name).
rationale: N+1 is the exact pattern: one query per item in a resolved field. The traps: connection-pooling limits concurrency (but queries still execute); replica-lag causes stale reads (not slow rendering). The

task: A 800MB file download over HTTP/2 blocks all concurrent requests on the same socket for 8-12 seconds. Reducing payload to 50MB eliminates the blocking. No frame prioritization settings changed.
rationale: TCP connection reuse problems manifest as connection exhaustion/timeouts, NOT payload-size-dependent blocking. GRpc deadlines cause cascading timeouts, not single-connection blocking. Deadlocks block

task: Serving pods crash with CUDA out-of-memory after 500 requests/sec is exceeded; error recovers when batch size drops from 64 to 16.
rationale: inference-batching-throughput is a textbook false positive: batch size adjustment sounds like batching optimization. But the issue is CUDA memory (explicit in task). The trap exploits the shared vocab

task: We're shipping a breaking change to the authentication system. We need to gradually roll it out: serve old auth to 95% of users, then ramp to 5%, 10%, 50% while monitoring errors, and flip a kill-switch instantly if error rates spike above threshold.
rationale: Blue-green deployment switches ALL traffic from old to new environment atomically. Feature-flag rollout serves different code to different percentages of requests. Both are zero-downtime with instant

task: User A loads an order record at 10:00:00 with status 'pending', User B loads the same record at 10:00:05 with status 'pending', User B changes it to 'shipped' and saves at 10:00:10, then User A changes it to 'refunded' and saves at 10:00:15—the final DB value is 'refunded' and shipping never happened.
rationale: The problem is LOST UPDATE—two users load the same record, both edit, and the second silently clobbers the first. Fix with version/etag + compare-and-swap. distributed-lock-redis enforces MUTUAL EXCLU

task: User creates account and immediately navigates to dashboard. Dashboard shows 'You have 0 followers' despite account creation success page confirming creation. Refresh after 3 seconds shows 'You have 1 follower' (from their own auto-follow). Database: 1 primary, 3 read-only replicas.
rationale: User's own write is not visible on immediate read—classic read-after-write inconsistency. The 3-second delay before visibility indicates replication lag to read replicas. A senior engineer recognizes:

task: A payment webhook writes to the ledger; the system checks the ledger to confirm. Because the write hasn't replicated to the read replica, it appears the payment failed. The webhook retries, charging the customer twice; the second charge is NOT deduplicated.
rationale: The ruling clues are 'appears the payment failed' (from stale read on replica lag) and 'charging twice' (dupe webhook processing). Distributed locks won't prevent the replica lag (it's data freshness,

task: Request lands at Service A with 10s deadline. Service A calls Service B (deadline 10s from A's start), which calls Service C. Service B takes 1s, C takes 9s. User sees timeout after 30+ seconds instead of failing fast at 10s.
rationale: gRPC deadline propagation ensures each hop subtracts elapsed time from the deadline. Without it, a 10s deadline doesn't reach Service C, causing hangs. rate-limiting-token-bucket throttles traffic, no

task: Inference latency jumps from 40ms to 4 seconds per request during peak traffic, even though GPU utilization is 55% and free VRAM is abundant. No errors in logs, no OOM messages, requests succeed.
rationale: High latency + low GPU utilization + no errors indicates the GPU is idle relative to incoming request rate. The model server is processing requests serially or in small batches, leaving the GPU underu

task: Your database schema needs a migration that is incompatible with code from 30 days ago. You want to roll out the change to 5% of customers for 24 hours, collect metrics, then proceed to 100% with an instant off switch if errors spike.
rationale: blue-green-deploy is tempting ('release strategy'), but is all-at-once cutover between two full environments, not percentage-based. hpa-autoscaling is tempting ('percentage' + 'scale'), but only auto-

task: You need to recompute customer LTV for 18 months of historical events; running this in parallel with prod traffic is causing prod pipeline to slow by 50%.
rationale: streaming-exactly-once is about correctness on restart (transactional sinks). kafka-consumer-lag is about real-time throughput health. distributed-lock-redis is about mutual exclusion across nodes. Th

task: A user changes their email address. Immediately after, they log in and still see the old email in their profile for 5-8 seconds, then it refreshes. Write/read hit same database.
rationale: Same database + 5-8 sec delay → read-after-write hitting replica lag. CDN doesn't cache profile data. HTTP caching wouldn't auto-refresh after 5-8 sec. Connection pool exhaustion would timeout, not re

task: Traffic volume jumps to 2000 req/sec during a flash sale. Application logs show 'waiting for available connection from pool' repeated thousands of times. Request latency climbs from 120ms p99 to 8s. The database-connection-pooling is configured with max: 15, min-idle: 2. Database CPU is at 30%.
rationale: N+1 is query logic (wouldn't cause 'waiting for available connection'), TCP reuse addresses ephemeral port exhaustion (different layer), and stream processing doesn't apply. The issue is pool size con

task: A customer email says: 'I see correlation-id ABC-789 in the response headers and your logs. Why is the API taking 3 seconds?' Logs from order-service, payment-service, and fulfillment-service all have the correlation-id, but the 3-second delay is opaque. We can't tell if it was serial waiting or one slow service.
rationale: Structured logging with correlation-id lets you find related logs, but doesn't show TIMING, LATENCY, or SERVICE DEPENDENCY GRAPH. Rate limiting and connection pooling are unrelated. distributed-tracin

task: Model server running on GPU exhausts system RAM after 2 hours, then crashes with OOMKilled despite 12GB free GPU VRAM. Logs show no Python memory growth. What is the issue?
rationale: OOMKilled is Kubernetes enforcing the pod's memory *limit* (pod-oomkilled), not GPU VRAM exhaustion. Free GPU VRAM rules out gpu-oom-inference. System RAM leak suggests CPU-side allocation (buffers, c

task: A single attacker IP is sending 10k reqs/sec to your API, bottlenecking your database. Meanwhile, normal users on other IPs see acceptable latency. You need per-client rate-limiting, not cluster-wide scaling.
rationale: hpa-autoscaling adds replicas but the attacker still consumes all resources, starving other users. distributed-lock-redis prevents concurrent execution, not abuse. database-deadlocks resolves lock con

task: Attackers are crafting requests that appear to originate from our payment provider, bypassing our webhook processing logic to trigger false refunds without legitimate authorization.
rationale: This is webhook authentication via HMAC signatures. webhook-signature-verification verifies that inbound webhooks really came from the provider. webhook-idempotency handles legitimate webhook retries,
